description: Warp-level execution: divergence, occupancy, and latency hiding. Stream-based concurrency: overlapping H2D transfers with kernel execution. Advanced synchronization: cooperative groups, dynamic parallelism, and barriers.
sources: books/Programming Massively Parallel Processors.pdf (Kirk & Hwu) — Ch4 CUDA Threads, Ch6 Performance Considerations §6.1 More on Thread Execution, §6.3 Dynamic Partitioning of SM Resources
books/cuda_by_example.pdf (Sanders & Kandrot) — Ch5 Thread Cooperation, Ch9 Atomics, Ch10 Streams

concepts.title: Warp Divergence
concepts.preciseDefinition: When threads within the same warp take different execution paths through an if/else branch or a loop with varying trip counts, the warp serializes each unique path: threads in the inactive path are predicated off while the active threads execute, then paths swap. Branch efficiency = (non-divergent branches / total branches). A 2-way divergence reduces effective throughput to 50%.
keyFacts: Divergence is resolved at the warp level: threads in the same warp must all complete before the warp re-converges
Pre-Volta: single program counter per warp causes serialization; Volta+ each thread has its own PC (independent thread scheduling)
Common bounds check (if (idx < n)) usually has high branch efficiency since only the last warp diverges
Loops: 'while (condition)' where condition differs per thread causes divergence each iteration
Nsight Compute metric: 'Branch Efficiency' — 100% = no divergence, <100% indicates serialization
commonMisconceptions: Any conditional causes warp divergence — divergence only occurs when threads in the same warp take different paths
Divergence in different warps is a problem — independent warps can take different paths without cost
Independent thread scheduling (Volta+) eliminates divergence overhead — it enables more flexible divergence handling but does not eliminate serialization

concepts.title: Warp Execution States
concepts.preciseDefinition: Each warp can be in one of three states: Active (resident in an SM, possibly waiting or executing), Eligible (active + all operands ready + no resource conflicts, can be issued next cycle), or Stalled (active but waiting for an outstanding operation: memory load, barrier, register dependency). The warp scheduler picks from eligible warps each cycle.
keyFacts: Stall reasons visible in Nsight Compute: Long Scoreboard (memory), Wait (short dependency), Barrier, No Instruction
Scoreboard stall: warp is waiting for a pending memory instruction to return data
High stall count is only a problem if no other eligible warps exist to fill the gap (low occupancy)
With sufficient occupancy, stalls are hidden by warp switching (latency hiding)
Issue efficiency = fraction of cycles where at least one instruction was issued
commonMisconceptions: Stalled warps are wasted computation — stalls are expected and hidden by switching to other warps
Issue efficiency of 100% means the kernel is optimal — a kernel can achieve 100% issue efficiency but still be memory-bound
Fewer stall cycles always means better performance — the goal is maximum throughput, not minimum stalls

concepts.title: Occupancy
concepts.preciseDefinition: The ratio of active warps per SM to the SM's maximum supported warps (64 on Hopper). Determined by the minimum of three resource limits: (1) threads per block vs max threads/SM, (2) registers/thread vs register file size, (3) shared memory/block vs total SM shared memory. Occupancy is a proxy for the SM's ability to hide latency: higher occupancy means more warps available to switch to when one stalls.
keyFacts: H100: max 64 warps/SM = 2048 threads/SM; if kernel uses 128 threads/block with 1 block/SM, occupancy = 4/64 = 6.25%
Occupancy calculator: cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor API
Sweet spot: typically 50-75% occupancy; marginal returns beyond 75% for compute-bound kernels
Theoretical vs achieved occupancy: theoretical assumes uniform resource use; achieved measured by Nsight Compute
Instruction-level parallelism (ILP) can compensate for low occupancy if each warp has many independent instructions
commonMisconceptions: 100% occupancy always means best performance — many compute-bound kernels peak at 50% occupancy
Occupancy and performance are linearly correlated — relationship is sublinear; diminishing returns
Low occupancy always hurts performance — for compute-bound kernels with high ILP, low occupancy is fine

concepts.title: Latency Hiding via Warp Switching
concepts.preciseDefinition: The GPU's primary strategy for tolerating long-latency operations: when a warp stalls (waiting for memory data, barrier, etc.), the warp scheduler immediately switches to another eligible warp at zero cost. By maintaining enough concurrent warps (governed by Little's Law: concurrency = latency × throughput), the SM keeps its execution units busy despite 400-cycle memory latencies.
keyFacts: Little's Law: concurrency needed = latency × throughput; for 400-cycle latency at 1 issue/cycle → 400 warps needed to fully hide
In practice: 400 cycles / 32 threads/warp = ~13 warps needed to hide one memory access per warp
GPUs have no hardware branch prediction or speculative execution — all latency hiding is via multi-threading
High ILP within a single warp can also hide dependency latencies without requiring many warps
Vasily Volkov's thesis (2016) formalized the ILP + occupancy balance for pre-Tensor Core GPUs
commonMisconceptions: More threads always hides more latency — adding threads beyond the saturation point has no benefit
Only memory latency matters for hiding — synchronization barriers, pipe hazards also cause stalls that need hiding
Latency hiding requires high occupancy — high ILP with low occupancy can achieve similar hiding

concepts.title: CUDA Streams
concepts.preciseDefinition: A sequence of CUDA operations (kernel launches, memcpy, events) that execute in order on the GPU. Operations in different streams can overlap if hardware resources permit. The default stream (stream 0) synchronizes with all other streams. Non-default streams enable concurrent kernel execution and overlap of computation with H2D/D2H transfers.
keyFacts: Create: cudaStreamCreate(&stream); Destroy: cudaStreamDestroy(stream)
Kernel in stream: kernel<<<grid,block,shmem,stream>>>(...)
cudaMemcpyAsync with non-null stream: requires host memory to be page-locked (pinned)
Events: cudaEventRecord(event, stream) inserts timestamp; cudaStreamWaitEvent for cross-stream dependencies
HyperQ (Kepler+): up to 32 hardware work queues allow truly concurrent multi-stream execution
commonMisconceptions: Operations in different streams always run concurrently — they may serialize if the GPU is fully occupied
Default stream is the same as a user-created stream — default stream synchronizes with all streams
cudaMemcpy is synchronous with all GPU operations — it only synchronizes the default stream

exercises.title: Overlap Transfers and Computation with Streams
exercises.whatItTeaches: Pipeline: split work into chunks, interleave H2D, kernel, D2H in separate streams. Pinned memory requirement for async transfers. CUDA events for timing. Achieving throughput gain via transfer/compute overlap.
exercises.starterCodeHint: for (int i = 0; i < NUM_STREAMS; i++) { cudaMemcpyAsync(d_in+i*chunk, h_in+i*chunk, ..., stream[i]); kernel<<<g,b,0,stream[i]>>>(d_in+i*chunk, d_out+i*chunk, chunk); cudaMemcpyAsync(h_out+i*chunk, d_out+i*chunk, ..., stream[i]); }
exercises.testCaseHint: Measure wall time with 1 stream vs 4 streams at N=10M; target: ~3x speedup vs sequential

concepts.title: Cooperative Groups
concepts.preciseDefinition: A CUDA programming model extension (CUDA 9+) that exposes explicit synchronization handles for subsets of threads at different granularities: thread_block (the whole CTA), tiled_partition<32> (one warp), tiled_partition<16> (sub-warp), and grid_group (entire grid via cooperative launch). Provides thread_group::sync(), thread_group::size(), thread_group::thread_rank() APIs.
keyFacts: #include <cooperative_groups.h>; namespace cg = cooperative_groups;
thread_block tb = cg::this_thread_block(); tb.sync(); // equivalent to __syncthreads()
tiled_partition<32> warp = cg::tiled_partition<32>(tb); for warp-level reduction
grid_group requires cooperative launch: cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel — device must support it
coalesced_threads() returns only the currently active threads (useful after divergence)
commonMisconceptions: Cooperative groups require cooperative launch — only grid_group requires it; thread_block and tiled_partition work in normal kernels
__syncthreads() and thread_block::sync() are different — they are equivalent
coalesced_threads() works across blocks — it only works within a block

concepts.title: Dynamic Parallelism
concepts.preciseDefinition: A CUDA feature (cc 3.5+) allowing kernels to launch child kernels from the device, without returning to the CPU. Enables adaptive algorithms (e.g., AMR, recursive algorithms like quicksort) where the GPU can self-direct work based on computed data. Child launches are placed in a child grid; parent can synchronize with cudaDeviceSynchronize() or a child stream.
keyFacts: Requires -rdc=true (relocatable device code) nvcc flag
Child launch has overhead: ~500 cycles for a child kernel launch from device
Useful when the number of work items is data-dependent and not known at host launch time
Not appropriate for fine-grained launch from individual threads — batch child launches from one thread
commonMisconceptions: Dynamic parallelism is free overhead — each child kernel launch from device costs ~500 cycles
Dynamic parallelism replaces streams — they are orthogonal; CDP enables device-side launches

coverageGaps: CUDA Graphs — cuda-samples/3_CUDA_Features/simpleCudaGraphs/ exists but no topic planned; relevant for production inference
Asynchronous barriers (__mbarrier) for Hopper/Ampere pipelining — referenced in programming guide but no exercise
Thread Block Clusters (Hopper cc9.0) — enables distributed shared memory across blocks; not yet in curriculum
